entry.title: SAP Cloud Identity Services integration with SAP Concur
entry.content:
Managing user identities across enterprise applications is a critical and often complex task. With SAP Cloud Identity Services, SAP Concur customers can centralize and streamline authentication and user provisioning in a secure and scalable way.

SAP Concur introduced a simplified Identity Management configuration between SAP Cloud Identity Services and SAP Concur. The setup is highly automated and covers both SAP IAS (Identity Authentication Service) and SAP IPS (Identity Provisioning Service) components.

This blog post will walk you through the prerequisites, considerations and implementation steps.

Introduction

SAP Cloud Identity Services is SAP's unified identity and access management solution, and it is a backbone of many SAP solutions. Furthermore, SAP is transforming its products and embedding many solutions with AI co-pilot Joule and SAP Cloud Identity Service constitutes a key architectural foundation.

SAP Cloud Identity Services consist of two components:

  • SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS) enables authentication and single sign-on for users in the cloud and simplifies user experience. SAP IAS is offered as a centralized security for authentication and SSO for protecting system to system communication. SAP IAS manages a Global User ID which uniquely identifies a user across SAP business applications and services.
  • SAP Identity Provisioning Service (IPS) manages identity lifecycle processes seamlessly by allowing you to provision identities/users from a source system to SAP Concur. SAP IPS is a centrally administered user management and identity provisioning service which is key for end-to-end integration. Global user ID (or, User UUID) is a unique ID that helps to integrate user-related data across system boundaries seamlessly.

SAP Cloud Identity Services in SAP Concur enablement

Before configuring SAP Cloud Identity Services in SAP Concur, there is one important prerequisite. You must work with the SAP Concur team to ensure your Concur tenant(s) is assigned to expected Customer Account. This is done by either contacting your SAP Concur Account Manager or by raising a support case. Upon the validation completion, SAP Concur personnel will assign a new permission to your Concur admin user.

The configuration process is straightforward and wizard guided. You will find below the high level overview:

  1. Submit a request to set up automated identity provisioning for your organization directly in your SAP Concur tenant
  2. Receive email confirmation once the request is completed
  3. Validate new Single Sign-On configuration in SAP Concur and new Application in your SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant

You can find below step by step guidance to configure SAP Cloud Identity Services in SAP Concur.

1. Log In to SAP Concur and navigate to Company Admin

Log in to SAP Concur using your Concur Admin account that has been previously shared with SAP Concur team. Navigate to Home -> Administration -> Company Admin

2. Open SAP Cloud Identity Services

Inside the Company Admin section, locate and click SAP Cloud Identity Services

3. Initiate the automation

Click Configure SAP Cloud Identity Services to launch the setup wizard

4. Choose Your Authentication Method

NOTE: This is the most important decision in the process, three options are available:

Option 1: Basic Authentication - To migrate your user authentication and password policy from SAP Concur to SAP Identity Authentication Service. You continue to manage your users in SAP Concur. Your users log in to Identity Authentication Service using their existing SAP Concur passwords and existing two-factor authentication one-time codes. Administrators need to change user passwords in Identity Authentication Service and continue to manage users and roles in SAP Concur.

Option 2: SAP Identity Authentication Service (recommended for new SAP Concur customers) - SAP Identity Authentication Service is configured to manage user authentication and user provisioning

Option 3: Other Authentication Service - SAP Identity Authentication Service is configured for your company. You need to configure this SAP Identity Authentication Service instance as a proxy in your third-party identity provider. This setup lets your users continue to use your existing login using your third party identity provider.

5. Select your SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant

Choose your SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant from the dropdown menu and click Next. Use Refresh if the list of tenants does not appear.

NOTE: Do not proceed further in case you already have SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant but you cannot find the tenant ID in the drop-down menu. It is recommended to raise an SAP Concur support ticket.

6. Review and Finish

Review your selected method and tenant, then click Finish, followed by Configure tenant in the popup window. The process may take several minutes to complete.

NOTE: Raise SAP Concur support ticket should the automation fail and provide the Execution ID from your SAP Concur screen.

Upon successful completion, you can review and validate new Single Sign-On setup in your SAP Concur. Navigate to Administration -> Company -> Authentication Admin -> Manage Single Sign-On. The newly configured Single Sign-on will be hidden by default.

You can also validate newly created Application in your SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant. Navigate to Applications & Resources -> Application and review the Bundled Application for SAP Concur.

Since May 2026 this automation also creates the SAP IPS provisioning jobs (Source and Target Systems under the Identity Provisioning tab) which allow you to provision users between SAP Concur and SAP Cloud Identity Services if required.

entry.title: Provisioning users for Selective access Joule Pilot on SAP Concur
entry.content:
SAP Concur allows using "Selective Access" for Joule, which enables you to test the SAP's Gen AI capabilities in your production environment using a restricted Joule Pilot User Group before a full, organization-wide rollout.

The “Selective Access” mechanism enables a controlled user experience, which is a key design principle for introducing AI features like Joule in enterprise applications.

Instead of enabling Joule for the entire organization at once, SAP Concur allows administrators to:

  • Restrict access to a predefined subset of users
  • Gradually introduce and validate AI functionality
  • Ensure that the feature behaves as expected under real production conditions

Once the solution has already been tested in production, the organization can proceed with higher confidence, validate business impact, better user acceptance. 

Pre-requisites :

Pilot Mode option in the Joule configuration settings at the company level must be enabled for Concur. Refer Enable Joule Pilot Mode for Your Company | SAP Help Portal

 

 

User Provisioning :

We would be focusing mostly on the provisioning part of the Joule setup which is required for selective access based on a predefined group.

There are 2 approaches - a fully automated end to end approach and a partially automated approach.

Set Up Pilot User Group — Option A vs. Option B

 Option A: Retrieve from ConcurOption B: Manual Creation in CIS
When to useConcur is already your user source and the group exists thereYou want to create and manage the pilot group directly in CIS (IAS)
Group originPulled from Concur → IAS via IPSCreated manually in IAS, then pushed to Concur

Option A — Retrieve group from Concur

  1. IPS Config (Concur → IAS): Set Concur as Source, IAS/Local Directory as Target. Extend transformation to include group replication and set filter: concur.group.filter = displayName eq Joule Pilot User
  2. Add Pilot Users in IAS: Navigate to Users & Authorizations → Groups → Joule Pilot User → add users.
  3. IPS config sync back (IAS → Concur): Set IAS as Source, Concur as Target. Filter with dds.group.filter = displayName eq Joule Pilot User. Run IPS job — group membership now controls Joule access automatically.

 Option B — Manual Creation of group in CIS

  1. Create Group manually in IAS – Joule Pilot User:
  2. Add Users: Assign pilot users to the group.
  3. IPS Config (LID/IAS -> Concur): Set Local Identity Directory as Source with filter on users assigned to user group "Joule Pilot User"
  4. IPS Config (Concur as Target): Set Concur as Target System with the standard user/group transformation → run IPS job to provision pilot user assignments to Concur.

 

We will walkthrough the simpler Option B where we would manually create ‘Joule Pilot User’ in CIS and then sync just the users assignments from IAS to Concur using IPS job. There are some pain points regarding connectivity to Concur which would be covered. 

However, if you want to setup or already have connection from Concur to IAS then you can also go with the option A. For the pilot involving just 1 group, option B is easier and faster.

1. Go to Users & Authorizations → Groups → create group named exactly ‘Joule Pilot User’ in IAS

2. Assign pilot users to the group – Joule Pilot User

Click ‘Add’ button in the group and search and add all the users required for this pilot.

 

3. IPS configuration for source system:

Go to Identity Provisioning -> Source systems

Configure Local Identity Directory as Source System

  1. Save and keep the default transformation
  2. In properties add:
NameValue
idds.group.filterdisplayName eq "Joule Pilot User"

This is important so that users are filtered based on the group – Joule Pilot User and not all the users are provisioned.

 

You can add other optional traces in properties.

ips.trace.created.entitytrue
ips.trace.created.entity.contentfalse
ips.trace.deleted.entitytrue
ips.trace.failed.entity.contentfalse
ips.trace.skipped.entitytrue
ips.trace.skipped.entity.contentfalse
ips.trace.updated.entitytrue
ips.trace.updated.entity.contentfalse

If you wish to replicate only limited number of users to Concur, user also filter idds.user.filter in property.

 

4. IPS configuration for Target System:

Go to Identity Provisioning -> Target systems

Configure Concur as Target System

  1. Source System: Local Identity Directory
  2. Replace transformation with below:
{
  "user": {
    "skipOperations": [
      "create",
      "delete"
    ],
    "mappings": [
      {
        "sourceVariable": "entityIdTargetSystem",
        "targetPath": "$.id"
      },
      {
        "constant": [
          "urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:core:2.0:User",
          "urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:User"
        ],
        "targetPath": "$.schemas"
      },
      {
        "sourcePath": "$.userName",
        "targetPath": "$.userName"
      },
      {
        "sourcePath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:User']['userUuid']",
        "optional": true,
        "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:User']['userUuid']"
      }
    ]
  },

  "group": {
    "ignore": false,
    "condition": "isAttributeWithOptionalPrefix($.displayName, concur.group.prefix) && isAttributeWithOptionalPrefix($['urn:sap:cloud:scim:schemas:extension:custom:2.0:Group']['name'], concur.group.prefix) && (isRegularGroup() || isApplicationSpecificGroup())",
    "mappings": [
      {
        "sourceVariable": "entityIdTargetSystem",
        "targetPath": "$.id"
      },
      {
        "constant": [
          "urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:core:2.0:Group",
          "urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:Group"
        ],
        "targetPath": "$.schemas"
      },
      {
        "sourcePath": "$.displayName",
        "targetPath": "$.displayName",
        "functions": [
          {
            "function": "replaceFirstString",
            "condition": "isAttributeWithMandatoryPrefix(@, concur.group.prefix)",
            "regex": "%concur.group.prefix%",
            "replacement": ""
          }
        ]
      },
      {
        "sourcePath": "$.members[*].value",
        "preserveArrayWithSingleElement": true,
        "optional": true,
        "targetPath": "$.members[?(@.value)]",
        "functions": [
          {
            "function": "resolveEntityIds"
          }
       ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

3. Properties:

Name

Value

Authentication

BasicAuthentication

concur.api.version

3

concur.company.domain

*************

concur.company.id

(Retreive this value from Concur. Steps mentioned below under 4.1. Generate company request token)

concur.datacenter

(Depends on your Concur tenant)

Concur.authorization.code(Credential type)

(Retreive this value from Concur. Steps mentioned below under 4.1. Generate company request token)

concur.support.patch.operation

true

concur.user.unique.attribute

userName

ips.trace.failed.entity.content

true

ProxyType

Internet

Type

HTTP

 

In case you are having SCIM v4 API supported on Concur, then you can put concur.api.version as 3. This is the connector version value. Please do not put 4 or any other value.

Value = 3 → SCIM v4 API

Value = 2 → Identity v4 API

Value = 1 (or not set) → User v1 API (deprecated)

 

4.1. Generate company request token – This part is additionally required to be done by Concur Administrator

4.1.2. In Concur, go to the Company Request Token Self-Service Tool page.

4.1.3.  Enter the App ID below for SAP IPS.

Identity Provider

App ID

SAP IPS

5bea7d57-6bc5-45ba-b5cf-91f04940fbf2

4.1.4.  Click Generate → a "Company Request Token Successfully Created" dialog appears.

4.1.5. Copy & save both values:

  • Company UUID
  • Company Request Token

4.1.6.  Use these two values in the 3. properties mentioned above and map as following:

concur.company.id - Company UUID

Concur.authorization.code - Company Request Token

NOTE : Token expires in 24 hours — if unused, repeat steps 1–4 to get a new one.

 

5.  After the first run of the job, Identity Provisioning fills in automatically a ‘refresh token’ as the value of the concur.refresh.token property. If a provisioning job has not been run for six months, you’ll again need to generate a new token. Below is shown what happens after first with an error.

 

But after the first run you would see that the property concur.authorization.code got replaced by concur.refresh.token. This means that IPS connectivity is established with Concur.

 

6. Now you can run the job for ‘Pilot User Group’ assignment synchronization to Concur and you should be able to see the updates under group.

 

Once all the steps are finalized, the Joule diamond icon becomes visible to authorized users in Concur, granting them full access to Joule's pilot features.

 

 

Conclusion :

By enabling Joule through SAP Concur’s Selective Access approach, organizations can introduce GenAI leveraging real production conditions. The step-by-step setup covers the provisioning activities required. There are a set of activities to be done additionally for connectivity which requires coordination between Concur admin and security admins and the steps have been detailed out. Once the pilot proves successful, organizations can confidently scale Joule adoption, backed by real validations and strong user acceptance.

 

References :

Joule Selective Access: Pilot Enablement | SAP Help Portal

Option B: Manual Creation of Joule Pilot User Group in CIS Tenant | SAP Help Portal

SAP Concur as target in Identity Provisioning SAP Concur | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Integrating SAP Concur with SAP IAG – Implementation Guide and Key Learnings
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP IAG Enterprise (Standard) edition provides out-of-the-box integration with SAP Concur for user provisioning and access governance. However, during implementation, it has been observed that certain areas require deeper understanding beyond standard documentation.

This blog covers:

  • Detailed configuration steps
  • Important technical considerations
  • Practical challenges faced during implementation

The intent is to provide a simple and practical guide for SAP IAG consultants working on SAP Concur integration.

 

Initial SAP IAG System Setup

Before starting the integration, ensure the following setup is completed:

  1. SAP BTP setup: Global Account → Subaccount → SAP IAG Enterprise Edition subscription (Test/Prod)
  2. Trust configuration is completed with SAP Cloud Identity Services (CIS), including attribute mapping
  3. Standard SAP IAG groups are created in SAP CIS, such as: IAG_USER, IAG_WF_MANAGER, IAG_WF_ADMIN, IAG_RAA_DEFAULT, etc.
  4. If the manager is used as an approval stage in access requests, the user’s manager must be assigned with the IAG_WF_MANAGER group so that it is automatically derived in the access request form. Otherwise, the requester needs to manually select the manager from users assigned to this group
  5. Role collection to CIS group mapping is configured (for example): CIAG_Access_Request, CIAG_Access_Analysis mapped to a custom CIS group like ZIAG_Access_Requestor.
  6. Access to SAP IAG with administrator privileges
  7. User data source is configured using SAP Identity Services Identity Directory or Identity Authentication Service via IPS_PROXY V2.
  8. Application parameters and master data are properly maintained
  9. User and group synchronization is configured using SCIM
  10. Mail server is configured for outbound emails (Destinations: parameters_destination and bpmworkflowruntime_mail)

 

SAP Concur to SAP IAG Integration and Configuration Steps

The following steps outline the end-to-end configuration required to integrate SAP Concur with SAP IAG after completing the initial system setup.

 

Step 1: User Provisioning Service V4 (UPSv4)

User Provisioning Service V4 (UPSv4) is an SAP Concur API framework that enables SAP IAG to automate user provisioning and lifecycle management in SAP Concur.

To use the User Provisioning Service (UPSv4) API, the client must have Concur Expense, Concur Travel, or both.

Clients using Concur Expense Professional/Premium Edition and/or Concur Request Professional/Premium Edition need to get UPS enabled through their Concur Client Executive (Sales Representative).

In addition, the client must subscribe to Client Web Services after signing the web services agreement. This will then be included as part of the SAP Concur contract.

For API details, refer to:

  • SAP API Hub – Concur User Provisioning
  • Concur Developer Documentation – User Provisioning v4

There are also certain limitations with UPS, which can be referred to in SAP Note:

  • SAP Note 3363157

 

Step 2: Configure OAuth2 authentication application and Generate company refresh token and UUID

Once the User Provisioning Service (UPSv4) is enabled, the next step is to configure OAuth2 authentication for SAP Concur integration.

This step establishes secure API-based authentication between SAP IAG and SAP Concur using OAuth2 credentials and tokens.

This involves two key activities:

Step 2.1: Configure OAuth2 Application

This helps to create authentication credentials (Client ID/Secret) and defines required API access scopes.

  • Create an OAuth2 authentication application in SAP Concur
  • Generate Client ID and Client Secret
  • Maintain the required grants and scopes

Step 2.2: Generate Company Token and UUID

This provides tenant-specific identifiers and tokens required for API communication with SAP Concur.

  • Generate the Company Request Token
  • Obtain: Token Service URL, Refresh Token, Company UUID

A detailed reference for this setup is available in the SAP Community blog by @tim_chapman24 

  • Introduction to Web Services for Concur Expense Authentication

Additional reference:

  • Concur Authentication API Documentation

Important Configuration Points

  • While configuring the OAuth2 application, ensure that the required grants and scopes are maintained correctly
  • Missing grant or scope may result in 403 (Forbidden) errors in provisioning logs

For SAP IAG integration, the following grants can be maintained:

  • refresh_token
  • password

For more details, refer to:

  • Refresh Token Grant
  • Password Grant

Scopes to be Maintained

Below are the commonly required scopes:

 

Scope

Scope Description

identity.user.core.read

Read user core data

identity.user.coresensitive.read

Read core sensitive data

identity.user.emails.verified.writeonly

Write access for verified email

identity.user.enterprise.read

Read user enterprise data

identity.user.coreenterprise.writeonly

Write access to all core and enterprise fields except external ID

identity.user.externalID.writeonly

Write access to external ID only

identity.user.ids.read

Read user ID data

identity.user.sap.read

Read a user’s SAP Global ID. Used for intra SAP service communication

identity.user.sap.writeonly

Write a user’s SAP Global ID. Used for intra SAP service communication

spend.user.general.read

View spend user information

spend.user.general.writeonly

Change spend user information

travel.user.general.read

Read general Travel data: Travel approval manager, Travel Name, Rule Class, Groups, Org Unit, Travel Custom Fields

travel.user.private.read

Read private Travel data: CRS Name/GDS Sync ID, Travel Name Remark, Gender

user.provision.read

Request status of a provisioning request

user.provision.write

Provision a user

 

Required Permissions in SAP Concur

Ensure that the Concur administrator has the following permissions:

Activity

Permission

Configure OAuth2 authentication Application

Web Services Administrator

Generate Company Request Token and Company UUID

Web Services Administrator

User Creation and Maintenance

Employee Administrator

Permission Assignment and Administration

Role Administrator

 

Step 3: Destination Setup in BTP Subaccount

This step defines the connectivity configuration in SAP BTP to enable SAP IAG to communicate with SAP Concur APIs.

Create a destination for SAP Concur in the BTP subaccount where the SAP IAG service is subscribed.

Maintain the destination with the following details:

Property Key

Values

Name

CONCUR (or any other preferred name)

Type

HTTP

Description

Concur Destination (or any other preferred description)

URL

<Enter SAP Concur API URL> For example: https://us.api.concursolutions.com  (Refer to https://me.sap.com/notes/2914977/E for Concur URLs)

ProxyType

Internet

Authentication

OAuth2RefreshToken

Use mTLS for token retrieval

<Unchecked>

Client ID

Enter the generated Concur Client ID in Step 2.1

Client Secret

Enter the generated Concur Client Secret in Step 2.1

Token Service URL Type

Dedicated

Token Service URL

Enter the SAP Concur token URL in Step 2.2

CompanyEntityCode

Registered Company Entity Code can be obtained from SAP Concur team

CompanyUUID

Generated Company UUID in Step 2.2 (will be used as prefix to user ID in SAP Concur)

RefreshToken

Generated refresh token in Step 2.2

Use default client truststore

<Checked>

 

Important Note: The Company Entity Code is used as a prefix for the user ID during provisioning in SAP Concur from SAP IAG. As a result, the user ID is created in the format: <user_ID>@<CompanyEntityCode>

 

Step 4: Create an Application in SAP IAG

This step registers SAP Concur as a managed application in SAP IAG to enable access governance and provisioning.

Log in to SAP IAG and navigate to: Administration → Application

Click on the ‘+’ icon to create a new application and maintain the following details:

Field

Value

Application Name

CONCUR (or Any other preferred name)

Description

SAP Concur Application (or any other preferred description)

Application Type

SAP Concur

HCP Destination

<BTP destination created above. Provide exactly same name of BTP destination.>

 

Mandatory Custom Fields

Once the application is created, SAP IAG automatically creates four mandatory custom fields.
These can be verified under: Administration → Custom Fields

Name

Description

Label

Input Type

Data Type

Field Length

Required

Allowed values

COUNTRY

Spend User Country

Country

Input Text

String

2

Yes

A two-letter country code defined in ISO 3166-1 alpha-2

F(e.g. GB for UK, AU for Australia, US for United States of America, etc.)

LOCALE

Spend User Locale

Spend-User Locale

Input Text

String

5

Yes

Required when using Spend User extension. Valid locale from the list of configured locales as defined in [RFC5646]. But here for Concur, the locale will be an ISO 639 language code with an ISO 3166 country/region code, separated by a underscore (e.g., en_US, en_AU, de_DE, etc.)

REIMBURSEMENT_CURRENCY

SpendUser Reimbursement Currency

Reimbursement Currency

Input Text

String

3

Yes

Valid three digit currency code in the list of system reimbursement currencies. Ex. AUD, USD, etc.

REIMBURSEMENT_TYPE

SpendUser Reimbursement Type

Reimbursement Type

Input Text

String

20

Yes

The reimbursement type for the user. Supported values: ACCOUNTS_PAYABLE, ADP_PAYROLL, CONCUR_PAY, OTHER.

 

Important Points

  • These custom fields will appear in the access request form for SAP Concur
  • All four fields are mandatory for both new and existing users
  • Values must be provided in the correct format, otherwise provisioning will fail

Even though SAP IAG provides an option to edit or deactivate these fields under Custom Fields, it is recommended not to change them, as they are required for Concur provisioning.

 

Additional Note

SAP is introducing additional custom fields to support more Concur attributes. You can track updates here: SAP Roadmap Explorer – SAP IAG Concur Enhancements

 

Step 5: Run Repository Sync Job

This step helps to synchronize users and access (roles/profiles) from SAP Concur into SAP IAG for governance processes.

After creating the application, run the Repository Sync job to fetch data from SAP Concur.

Navigate to Administration → Job Scheduler and execute the Repository Sync job for the SAP Concur application

Once the job is completed successfully, user and access data from SAP Concur are synchronized to SAP IAG.

You can verify the synchronized data in:

  • Maintain User Data
  • Access Maintenance (use filters for SAP Concur)

User Data

  • SAP Concur user ID is displayed in the format: <user_id>@<CompanyEntityCode>
  • The <user_id> corresponds to the login name maintained in SAP CIS

Access Data

Under Access Maintenance, two types of access are available for SAP Concur:

  1. Concur Product: Represents Concur profiles such as Expense, Invoice, Reporting, Request, and Travel
  2. Single Roles: Represents authorization roles such as EXP_APPROVER, EXP_ATTENDEE_ADMIN, EXP_CONFIG_ADMIN_CLIENT, etc.

Both access types can be requested via SAP IAG. Additional attributes such as: Business Process, Subprocess, Criticality, Alias, Assignment Approvers etc. can be maintained to control workflow routing and approval determination.

Note:
Only users assigned with the IAG_RAA_DEFAULT group are available for role assignment approval.

 

Step 6: Configure Access Risk Ruleset

This step helps to enable risk analysis by importing and activating SAP Concur-specific segregation of duties rules.

For risk analysis, the standard GLOBAL ruleset for SAP Concur needs to be requested.

  • Create an SAP Support case for component: GRC-IAG-AA (Access Analysis Service)
  • Provide required access (IAS Administration) to SAP to upload the ruleset

Once the ruleset is uploaded:

  • A business function group SAP_CONCUR is created
  • All SAP Concur-related risks are available under this group

Ruleset Behavior

  • SAP Concur roles are standard and cannot be customized
  • Risks are defined based on conflicting role combinations

Example:

Post ruleset setup, validate the risk count against the business function group and run the Access Analysis job from the Job Scheduler.

After completion of Access Analysis job User-level risks can be checked in ‘Access Analysis’ application and role-level risks can be checked in ‘Access Maintenance’ application.

             

Step 7: User ID mapping

This step aligns user identities across systems to avoid duplicates and ensure accurate license consumption.

User ID mapping is important for license optimization in SAP IAG. The Monitored User List in Maintain User Data shows users consuming IAG licenses

Key Consideration

SAP Concur uses a different user ID format: <user_ID>@<prefix>

If multiple applications are integrated with SAP IAG:

  • The same user may appear as different users due to different ID formats
  • This can lead to duplicate license consumption

SAP IAG supports auto user ID mapping for most applications Refer: SAP Note 3553352 (Auto-Generated Entries in User ID Mapping) however, SAP Concur is not supported for auto-mapping

Hence to avoid duplicate license consumption user ID mapping must be maintained manually. This requires additional monitoring as part of ongoing operations for new user creation and also increases administrative effort.

 

How to Maintain Mapping

  • Navigate to: Administration → Maintain User ID Mapping
  • Mapping can be maintained: Individually or via Bulk upload through CSV file
  • Master User ID → Login name from user source and Mapped ID → SAP Concur User ID

 

Additional Notes

  • Other IAG services (Access Request, Workflow, Role Design, Access Analysis, Access Certification) work similarly to other applications
  • Privileged Access Management (PAM) Service is not supported for SAP Concur

 

Key Learnings and Limitations

Based on implementation experience, below are some important points to be aware of:

1. Provisioning Behavior (Asynchronous Processing)

Provisioning in SAP Concur works in an asynchronous manner.

  • Step 1: When the access request is submitted, the first run of the provisioning job shows the status as “In Process”
  • Step 2: In the next job run, the final status is updated as “Successful” or “Failed” based on the actual result

So, the provisioning result is not available immediately in the first run.

2. Manual User ID Mapping Required

As SAP Concur does not support automated user ID mapping:

  • Manual mapping is required in SAP IAG
  • Continuous monitoring is needed for new users

Without this, duplicate users may be created, leading to additional license consumption.

3. Mandatory Custom Fields

All four custom fields are mandatory:

  • COUNTRY
  • LOCALE
  • REIMBURSEMENT_CURRENCY
  • REIMBURSEMENT_TYPE

If any field is left empty or deactivated, provisioning will fail.

4. No Validation for Custom Fields

Custom fields accept only specific values (mentioned in step 4) and formats (case-sensitive).

However:

  • Validation is not enforced at the request submission stage, which may require additional checks during provisioning
  • Users can submit incorrect values
  • Request goes through full approval workflow
  • Failure happens only during provisioning job execution.

In addition:

  • Logs may require additional analysis to identify the exact issue
  • Administrators need to analyze logs to find the problem

 5. No Pre-population of Custom Fields

  • Custom fields need to be maintained for both new and existing users
  • There is no pre-filled data even for existing users

This requires proper end-user training before rollout.

 6. Partial User Creation in case of errors

If incorrect values are provided in custom fields user may still get created in SAP Concur but no profiles or roles are assigned

Such users are difficult to find using standard user search and may require support from SAP Concur functional team to verify at database level.

Users who exist in SAP Concur but do not have any profiles (Expense, Travel, etc.) will still appear in Maintain User Data and will be counted for SAP IAG licensing.

7. No Bulk Provisioning Support

SAP IAG currently does not support bulk user provisioning/deprovisioning for SAP Concur.

Recommended approach:

  • Use SAP ICS (Integration with SAP Concur Solutions) / SAP IPS for user creation with basic roles and/or user account termination/deactivation.
  • Use SAP IAG only for role-based access requests

8. Option for HR-Driven Provisioning

Integration with SuccessFactors can be used to trigger user creation/termination and manage lifecycle and provisioning automatically.

9. Refresh Token Handling

  • Refresh token used in BTP destination has a validity of 6 months
  • SAP IAG / SAP IPS automatically refreshes the token during provisioning job execution

No manual intervention is required in normal scenarios.

10. Standard Risk Rules May Need Adjustment

The Global ruleset for SAP Concur includes some risks which may not be relevant because SAP Concur has built-in controls for certain scenarios to prevent risks.

Such risks can be reviewed with business and deactivated, if required.

 

Overall, the integration is manageable, but attention to detail is important, especially around custom fields and user mapping. This guidance is based on practical implementation experience, highlighting what worked well and the challenges encountered during execution.

Going forward, SAP may address some of these challenges, such as improvements in custom field handling, automated user ID mapping, more relevant global rulesets, and clearer provisioning logs, which should simplify the integration further.

If you have faced similar challenges or have additional insights, feel free to share your experience in the comments.

entry.title: Simplified and Flexible SAP Concur Integration for Service Order Expense Posting
entry.content:
Introduction  

SAP Concur integration enables you to post travel-related expenses directly against service order items. The Concur Relevant indicator at the service order item level controls which service order items are transferred to SAP Concur—only items marked as relevant and in Released status are eligible for export as cost objects for expense postings. 

For organisations where service orders are predominantly generated from solution orders, expense items (fixed price or non-billable) arrive already in released status. The Concur relevance field locks immediately, blocking any manual intervention. Combined with the inability to enable phased rollouts, set intelligent defaults, or control integration at a granular level, these limitations demanded a fundamental rethink. 

SSCUI-based configuration transforms Concur integration from manual selection to intelligent, rule-driven automation. By defining rules based on transaction type, sales organization, product, and item category, the system automatically determines Concur relevance at creation time—before release status locks the field. This enables controlled adoption across divisions, automatic flagging based on business logic, and seamless integration for solution-order-driven workflows. 

This guide covers the complete implementation journey: SSCUI configuration strategies, end-to-end UI workflows from solution order creation through expense reporting and FI posting, and technical architecture.

 

SSCUI Configuration Guide 

SSCUI (Self-Service Configuration UI) is your control center for automating Concur integration. Instead of manually checking boxes on every service order, you set up rules once, and the system automatically handles Concur flagging based on your business logic. 

 

How SSCUI Works: Decision Flow Overview   

When you open SSCUI configuration, you'll see a table with these below columns. 

The diagram above illustrates how SSCUI-based Concur integration determines whether service order expense items should be processed through Concur 

When a service order item is created, the system follows this logic: 

  1. SSCUI Evaluation: System queries the configuration table using the Service Order attributes 
  2. Concur Relevance Check:
    • If Concur Relevance = YES: 
      • System checks the "Is Default Flag" 
        • If Default = YES: Concur Relevance checkbox on UI is automatically checked 
        • If Default = NO: Concur Relevance checkbox on UI remains unchecked 
      • Field editability is determined by "Is Editable" flag 
    • If Concur Relevance = NO: 
      • Concur Relevant field is disabled (greyed out)   
      • Item is not eligible for Concur processing 
  3. Order Save: Service order is saved with the determined Concur relevance status  

Understanding the SSCUI Configuration Screen 

When you open SSCUI configuration, you'll see a table with these below columns. 

Think of SSCUI configuration as creating "IF-THEN" rules: 

IF an item matches these characteristics → THEN apply these Concur settings 

Every rule you create has two parts: 

Part 1: Matching Criteria (The "IF" Part) 

These fields define which items the rule applies to: 

Field 

What It Means 

Example 

Transaction Type 

Type of service order 

SVO1 (T&M), SVO2 (Fixed Price) 

Sales Organization 

The sales org assigned to the order 

1010, 1070, etc. 

Distribution Channel 

Channel through which the service is delivered 

10 (Direct), 20 (Partner) 

Division 

Business division within the organization 

01 (Field Service), 02 (Projects) 

Product ID 

The specific product or service being ordered 

SRV_02 

Material Group 

Product grouping for categorization 

SERVICES, MAINTENANCE 

Item Category 

Type of expense item  

SRVE (Fixed Price Expense), non-billable 

 

Special Character: * (Asterisk) = "Any" 

  • Sales Org = * means "applies to all sales orgs" 
  • Product = * means "applies to all products" 

Part 2: Control Flags (The "THEN" Part) 

These fields define what happens when an item matches:  

Flag 

Usage 

Options 

Concur Relevance 

Determines whether items matching the configuration criteria are eligible for Concur integration. 

YES = Enable / NO = Disable 

Default Flag 

Determines whether a service order item should be marked as Concur-relevant by default at the time of service order creation. 

YES = Auto-check / NO = Leave unchecked 

Is Editable 

This option controls whether users can manually override the system's determination 

YES = Users can edit / NO = Locked 

Accessing SSCUI Configuration 

User Actions: 

  1. Login to the system using the user BPC_EXPERT  
  2. Open the App Manage Your Solution 
  3. Click on Configure Your Solution 
  4. Provide Application area Service and Sub Application Area Integration 
  5. Find the SSCUI named Integration with SAP Concur (SSCUI ID 107355) 
  6. Click on the line to open the item detail page 
  7. Click on Configure under Configuration Steps  
  8. Click on New Entries button 
  9. Use the value help(F4) to select valid values for each field 
  10. Select values for all seven matching criteria fields 
  11. Use specific values for targeted rules, or `*` for broader rules 
  12. Set Concur Relevance Flag = YES or NO 
  13. Set Default Flag = YES or NO   
  14. Set Is Editable Flag= YES or NO 
  15. Click on Save 

     

Understanding Configuration Priority: Which Rule Wins? 

When you configure multiple rules in SSCUI, you might have situations where a service order item matches more than one rule. So which rule does the system apply? The answer lies in rule specificity—the more specific the rule, the higher its priority. 

  • How the System Determines Priority : The configuration columns have weightage from left to right—the leftmost columns carry the highest weight, and the weight decreases as you move right. When evaluating rules, the system prefers the rule with the most specific matches (fewest * entries). 
  • Column Weightage (High to Low): Transaction Type → Sales Org → Distribution Channel → Division → Product ID → Material Group → Item Category 
  • Real-World Example: Two Matching Rules 

         You have two configuration entries

  • Rule 1 (More Specific): Type: SVO1 | Sales Org: 1010 | Division: 10| Channel: 00 → Concur Relevance: YES | Default Flag : YES | Is Editable: YES  
  • Rule 2 (Less Specific): Type: SVO1 | Sales Org: 1010 | Division: * | Channel: * → Concur Relevance: YES | Default Flag : YES | Is Editable: NO  
  • Service order created with: Type SVO1, Sales Org 1010, Division 10, Channel 00 
  • Result: System applies Rule 1 because it has specific values for Division and Channel (not *). The item will be Concur relevant, auto-checked, and editable (following Rule 1's setting). 

 

End-to-End Process Flow in the UI 

This section walks you through the complete end-to-end process in the user interface, starting from Solution Order creation through Service Order processing with automatic SAP Concur integration.

The same configuration also works seamlessly when the process starts with a Service Quotation or a Service Order.

Step 1: Create Solution Order with Service Items 

The journey begins with creating a solution order that will generate service orders with expense items. 

User Actions: 

  1. Navigate to the Manage Solution Orders application   
  2. Click "Create" to start a new solution order.  
  3. Enter the below required header informations:
    • Sold-to Party 
    • Sales Organization 
    • Distribution Channel 
    • Division 
    • Description

       

  4. Add the expense items and change the item category as fixed price
  5. Click on Release All items
  6. Click on Save 
  7. Click on Refresh 
  8. Go to the Transaction History and check Service order created
  9. From the Transaction navigate to the app Manage Service Orders

     

  10. Click on Edit 
  11. Check the expense item and ‘Concur Relevant’ flag is set by default Note : To display the Concur Relevant column in the item table, choose the Personalise ( ) icon. On the Columns tab, select the Display checkbox for Concur Relevant. Choose Save to apply your changes.

Step 2:  Service Order Integration with Concur 

For more detailed information, please refer to the Help documentation, specifically  Service Order Integration with Concur section.

User Actions: 

  1. Login to the S/4 HANA system using user CONF_EXPERT_BUS_NET_IN
  2. Open the App ‘Concur Integration Setup’ 
  3. Check the communication Arrangement  
  4. Select the required system line  

     

  5. Click on Other cost objects -> Service order Item-> Manual Export 
  6. Enter the Service Document ID in the Service Order Header. Then, under System, clear (untick) the Test Mode checkbox. 

     

  7. Click on Execute 
  8. Check the overview and find the service order item is successfully replicated to the concur system 

     

Step 3 : App ‘Concur Communication Monitor’ 

User Actions:

  1. Open the App ‘Concur Communication Monitor’ 
  2. Click on number of ‘successful objects’ in the line CostObjects/Service order items 
  3. Check the Service Document number which is successfully replicated in the concur system 

Step 4:  Login to the concur system 

User Actions: 

  1. Click on create or Create Expense Report 
  2. Fill the below details in the create New Report 

    Report Name 

    Report Start Date 

    Type : Select the (SI) Service Order Item 

    Cost Object ID : Select the successfully replicated Service Order ID-Item No- type 

     

  3. Click on Create Report 
  4. Check Report created  
  5. Manually add expense in the report by clicking ‘Add Expense’
  6. Select the type of Expense
  7. Provide the below details :

    Transaction Date , Amount, City of purchase 

  8. Click on Save Expense 
  9. Click on Submit Report 
  10. Again click on Submit Report in the pop up  
  11. Click on Accept & Continue 
  12. Check Report Submitted 
  13. Check the created Report Number 

Step 5: Go back to the S/4 HANA system 

After creating the report in the Concur system, check the Report Number in S/4 HANA system 

User Actions: 

  1. Open the application Concur Posting Documents Monitor 
  2. Click on Feedback Loop->Step-by-Step->Import Documents from the Queue 
  3. Select All documents and click on Enter

     

  4. Check the previously created Report Number is present in this application and Document status is New 
  5. Click on Feedback Loop->Step-by-Step->Send Acknowledgement 
  6. Verify the Acknowledgement checkbox is Ticked means it is acknowledged 
  7. Click on Feedback Loop->Step-by-Step->Post 
  8. Check the posting message below 
  9. Verify the document status ‘Successful’ 
  10. Click on the Document ID 
  11. Check the selected posting document and click on cancel   
  12. Click on Feedback Loop->Step-by-Step->Send Confirmation 
  13. Check the below message after posting confirmation 
  14. Verify the confirmation Sent Checkbox is Ticked 

     

Step 6: Go back to the concur System

User Actions: 

  1. Open the previously posted Report and check the report status as ‘Processing Payment’ 

     

  2. Now, Refresh it and verify the report status ‘Sent for Payment’. So after posting confirmation, the report status became ‘Sent for Payment’ 

     

  3. Click on Audit Trail 

     

  4. Check the Document number which posted successfully  

Note : Service order items marked as Concur Relevant are excluded during  service confirmation creation, so you cannot create a service confirmation for these items.

entry.title: A New-Era of Learning for Support from SAP - Support Accreditation 2026 Release is coming soon!
entry.content:
Support Accreditation has long been the foundation for enabling customers, partners, and employees to successfully engage with support from SAP.

With its 2026 release, the program undergoes its most significant evolution to date - redesigned to reflect modern learning approaches, the latest advancements in SAP’s support services, and extensive feedback from customers and learners.

Built for a new era of AI-driven, proactive, and personalized support, the updated experience focuses on one clear goal: enabling users to resolve issues faster, make better decisions, and maximize the value of SAP. 

Stay tuned for more updates coming your way in the next 10 days!

entry.title: SAP Integration with Concur Solutions: Substitution Rules
entry.content:
This blog post highlights the newest enhancements to substitution rules in SAP Integration with Concur Solutions (also referred to as ICS or Native Integration) delivered with SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud 2608. For example, you can now use the Concur Substitution Rules app to define rules for journal entry header fields. To make things practical, I’ll also walk you through an example of configuring Concur-specific substitution rules.

What’s new in the Manage Concur Substitution Rules app?

  • Additional source fields are now available for defining substitution rules. In fact, nearly every non-technical field from an expense report JSON is now available.
  • Additional target fields are available for defining substitution rules at the journal entry line item level.
  • Substitution rules can now be defined at the journal entry header level as well.

The graphic below shows the list of fields which are available at the line item level:

Target Fields at Line Item Level

 

The following fields are available at the header level:

Target Fields at Header Level

 

 

 

Example Scenario

Let’s assume the following requirements:

  • My organization wants to adjust the journal entry header text and the document reference ID to indicate that the journal entry was created for the CEO’s expense report.
  • My organization wants to generate statistical postings on the traveler’s home cost center whenever travel costs are not allocated to a cost center.

To meet these requirements, I need to configure two substitution rules in the Manage Concur Substitution Rules app.

Rule 1: Adjusting the journal entry header

I create a rule for the business context "Expense Report – Header Level". As a precondition, I check whether the traveler’s employee ID matches the CEO’s employee ID. I then define the following substitutions:

  • The AccountingDocumentHeaderText should be substituted with the constant value "CEO’s Travel Expenses".
  • The DocumentReferenceID should be substituted with a concatenation of the expense report number and the constant value "CEO", separated by a slash (/).

The graphic illustrates how to configure this rule:

Rule for Adjusting the Journal Entry Header

 

 

Rule 2: Adjusting the journal entry line items

Next, I create a rule for the business context "Expense Report – Line Item Level" with two preconditions:

  1. The rule should only apply when the expense isn’t allocated to a cost center. Since an expense can be split across multiple cost objects—each generating a separate journal entry line item—I need to evaluate this at the allocation level. In my setup, this information is stored in CUSTOM3 on the expense entry allocation level.
  2. The rule should only apply to expense G/L account line items. ICS adds cost object information only to expense G/L account line items, and since I expect only debit postings on these accounts, I restrict the rule to item type EXP (Expense Account Debit).

In my organization’s setup, the employee’s home cost center is stored in CUSTOM13 of the employee master data record within Concur. This value is available during expense report posting via the source field EmployeeCustom13Code. Therefore, I define that EmployeeCustom13Code should substitute the content of the CostCenter field.

The graphic illustrates how to configure this rule:

Rule for Adjusting the Journal Entry Line Items

 

 

Result: What Does the Journal Entry Look Like?

Let’s take a look at the CEO’s expense report, which contains one expense whose allocation is split evenly between a cost center and a WBS element. With the new substitution rules applied, you can observe the following changes in the resulting journal entry and compare them to a standard ICS posting:

Journal Entry Header

 

Journal Entry Line Item

 

Feel free to leave a comment if you have questions or want to share feedback!
